perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that the other, because of the nature of the Confidential Information, would suffer irreparable harm in the event of a material breach of the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement in that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate for such a breach, and that in the event of any material breach or threatened mat erial breach by a party receiving Confidential Information from the other party of any such provisions, a party shall be entitled, in addition to such other legal or equitable remedies which might be available, to injunctive relief in any court of competen t jurisdiction against the threatened material breach or continuation of any such material breach without showing or proving any actual damages sustained by it. Moreover, any such award of relief to the discloser of such Confidential Information shall inc lude recovery of all actual and reasonable costs associated with enforcement of this Agreement including, without limitation, attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that the other, because of the nature of the Confidential Information, would suffer irreparable harm in the event of a material breach of the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement in that monetary damages would be adequate to compensate for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause regarding injunctive relief and attorney's fees limits the remedies available to the disclosing party in case of a breach. This is not a typical clause to remove in a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) Article 63
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR Article 63 governs injunctive relief. While it doesn't mandate a specific clause in contracts, removing a party's explicit right to seek injunctive relief may contradict the availability of such remedies under NY law, particularly when irreparable harm is acknowledged. Injunctive relief is a standard remedy in trade secret cases.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly provides for injunctive relief and recovery of attorney's fees in case of a material breach. The changed text removes the clause regarding injunctive relief and attorney's fees, which limits the remedies available to the disclosing party. The Law Explanation states that removing a party's explicit right to seek injunctive relief may contradict the availability of such remedies under NY law, especially when irreparable harm is acknowledged. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 6301, states that a preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where the defendant threatens or is about to do an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights. While the law doesn't mandate the inclusion of an injunctive relief clause in the contract, removing it when the original contract acknowledged irreparable harm weakens the available remedies and contradicts the intent of providing sufficient protection against breaches of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: This Confidentiality Agreement and performance thereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, excluding its conflicts of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Confidentiality Agreement and performance thereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflicts of laws rules.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law without justification can create uncertainty and potential conflicts, especially if the parties are primarily located and operate outside of Delaware.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law in contracts over $250,000 under certain circumstances. While the original clause aligns with this, changing the governing law to Delaware might be seen as an attempt to circumvent New York law, especially if the transaction and parties are primarily based in New York and the contract value exceeds $250,000. This contradicts the principle that choice of law clauses should have a reasonable relationship to the agreement.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

perturbation.justification: The change from New York to Delaware law potentially contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401. The original text aligned with the possibility of applying New York law, as permitted under specific conditions (contracts over $250,000). By switching to Delaware law, especially if the contract and parties are primarily based in New York, the modification might be viewed as circumventing New York law. Snippet 1 and 2 both state that 'the parties to any contract...covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars...may agree that the law of this state shall govern.' This indicates that New York law could be legitimately chosen. Changing the governing law creates a conflict, particularly if the conditions of NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 are met, and there is no clear reason or relationship to Delaware.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party makes or intends to make any warranty or representation of any kind concerning the Confidential Information, except each party represents and warrants that it has the right to disclose Confidential Information to Receiving Party. Confidentia l Information is provided only for discussion purposes, is provided on an “As Is” basis, and Receiving Party relies on Confidential Information at its own risk.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party makes or intends to make any warranty or representation of any kind concerning the Confidential Information. Confidentia l Information is provided only for discussion purposes, is provided on an “As Is” basis, and Receiving Party relies on Confidential Information at its own risk.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception related to the right to disclose, the modified text might imply that a party could disclose information they don't have the right to disclose, which could be considered a breach of contract and a violation of trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY CLS CPLR § 4548 doesn't directly address disclosure rights, it protects trade secrets. Removing the warranty regarding the right to disclose creates an ambiguity. This ambiguity increases the chances the disclosing party might share confidential information to which it does not have rights. Although unintentional, such action could then expose the receiving party to liability and the disclosing party to damages if trade secrets are misappropriated from a third party and shared.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly warrants the right to disclose confidential information. The changed contract removes this warranty, which, according to the perturbation explanation, means a party could disclose information they don't have the right to disclose. The law explanation states that while NY CLS CPLR § 4548 doesn't directly address disclosure rights, it protects trade secrets, and removing the warranty regarding the right to disclose creates an ambiguity that could expose the receiving party to liability. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is inaccessible, and Snippet 2 is non-existent, the potential for trade secret misappropriation, as explained by the LLM, resulting from the removal of the warranty, supports the existence of a contradiction and potential liability.
